elements.sent-2411-002: One of the main responsibilities of a Transmission System Operator (TSO) operating an electric grid is to maintain a designated frequency (e.g., 50 Hz in Europe).
elements.sent-2411-003: To achieve this, TSOs have created several products called frequency-supporting ancillary services.
elements.sent-2411-004: The Frequency Containment Reserve (FCR) is one of these ancillary service products.
elements.sent-2411-005: This article focuses on the TSO problem of determining the volume procured for FCR.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, we investigate the potential benefits and impact on grid security when transitioning from a traditionally static procurement method to a dynamic strategy for FCR volume.
elements.sent-2411-007: We take the Nordic synchronous area in Europe as a case study and use a diffusion model to capture its frequency development.
elements.sent-2411-008: We introduce a controlled mean reversal parameter to assess changes in FCR obligations, in particular for the Nordic FCR-N ancillary service product.
elements.sent-2411-009: We establish closed-form expressions for exceedance probabilities and use historical frequency data as input to calibrate the model.
elements.sent-2411-010: We show that a dynamic dimensioning approach for FCR has the potential to significantly reduce the exceedance probabilities (up to $37\%$) while keeping the total yearly procured FCR volume the same as compared to the current static approach.
elements.sent-2411-011: Achieving this stability demands precise interventions, for which TSOs have developed various frequency-supporting ancillary services tailored to address specific fluctuations and contingencies.
elements.sent-2411-012: These ancillary services vary between synchronous areas, as each grid has unique operational characteristics that shape the design of frequency-support mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the Continental Europe synchronous area, for example, TSOs employ three primary types of frequency containment and restoration services.
elements.sent-2411-014: Each service type has distinct response times, operating conditions, and purposes.
elements.sent-2411-015: The fastest is the Frequency Containment Reserve (FCR) service and is also called `primary reserve' or `regulating reserve'.
elements.sent-2411-016: FCR-providers can bid in a market dedicated to the procurement of this service to provide power (i.e., capacity) for up or down regulation within 30 seconds, with the goal of containing the frequency process.
elements.sent-2411-017: The second ancillary service product is the automatic Frequency Restoration Reserve (aFRR) and is also called `secondary reserve' or `operating reserve'.
elements.sent-2411-018: Here, providers should be able to provide a full response within 5 minutes, with the goal of restoring the frequency back to 50 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-019: The third and `slowest' ancillary service product is the manual Frequency Restoration Reserve (mFRR) and is also called `tertiary reserve' or `replacement reserve'.
elements.sent-2411-020: Here, providers should provide a full response within 15-20 minutes, and the goal is also to restore the frequency back to 50 Hz \cite{EnerginetPreQ , \cite{EnerginetOutlook .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Another key synchronous area in Europe is the Nordic synchronous area, which serves as the case study for this paper.
elements.sent-2411-022: This grid connects the electricity networks of Norway, Sweden, Finland, and the eastern part of Denmark.
elements.sent-2411-023: Due to its smaller size relative to the Continental Europe synchronous area (which spans 24 countries), the Nordic area experiences more pronounced frequency impacts from power disturbances, resulting from its inherently lower grid inertia~\cite{MEHIGAN,EntsoeInertia .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The Nordic TSOs employ additional ancillary services designed to provide rapid frequency response, particularly for primary reserves~\cite{EnerginetPreQ,EnerginetOutlook .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In particular, the Nordic TSOs utilize a specialized service called Fast Frequency Response (FFR) to immediately decelerate disturbances when grid frequency begins to deviate.
elements.sent-2411-026: In addition, the familiar Frequency Containment Reserve (FCR) service is used here but is adapted to the Nordic area’s needs: it differentiates between FCR-N for ``normal'' frequency deviations (49.9–50.1 Hz) and FCR-D for ``disturbed'' frequencies (below 49.9 Hz or above 50.1 Hz).
elements.sent-2411-027: Meanwhile, aFRR and mFRR reserves are also implemented in the Nordics, similar to their use in continental Europe, with their alignment facilitated by the pan-European projects PICASSO (for aFRR) and MARI (for mFRR) \cite{EnerginetPreQ,EnerginetOutlook .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Figure \ref{fig:ASactivation illustrates the activation timelines for these ancillary services within the Nordic area.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{White-Ancillary-svcs-activation-DK2-ENG.png} \caption{Activation times of ancillary services in Nordic synchronous area. Figure is borrowed from \cite{EnerginetOutlook}.} \label{fig:ASactivation}
elements.sent-2411-029: In the Nordics, the aFRR service is deployed proactively alongside FCR-N to address rising imbalances.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, with increased grid volatility due to electrification and a higher share of renewable generation, TSOs may soon face challenges maintaining frequency within safety margins \cite{Homan .}
elements.sent-2411-031: One possible strategy would be to increase the procured aFRR volumes.
elements.sent-2411-032: Although this could help moderate frequency volatility to some extent, the need for more responsive ancillary services, such as FCR, will grow as renewable penetration rising also yields a lower total inertia, thus shortening the time available to stabilize the grid during unexpected disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this study, we examine the risk implications for grid security of adopting a more dynamic approach to dimensioning the FCR-N volume, keeping FCR-D unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-034: Currently, the Nordic TSOs take a static approach by procuring a constant volume of 600 MW for both up and down-regulation of FCR-N each hour throughout the year.
elements.sent-2411-035: This static volume of FCR-N is based on historical estimates of random load variations amounting to ±1\% of the 60 GW system load~\cite{Entsoe .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our primary contribution is to challenge this static approach and investigate the impact of a dynamic FCR-N dimensioning method, wherein the volume procured varies hourly.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our findings indicate that this dynamic dimensioning strategy can reduce the likelihood of frequency deviations exceeding the $\pm 100$ mHZ band by as much as 37\% with respect to the current static approach, all while keeping the same total annual FCR-N volume procurement.
elements.sent-2411-038: To provide these insights, we model the frequency dynamics and the effect of FCR activation using a diffusion process framework.
elements.sent-2411-039: Recent studies have applied similar stochastic processes to various power system state variables, such as line loads, line frequencies, and nodal voltages \cite{AdeenMilano, Deadbands, Nesti, PatchZwart, Homan, Kruse, Sheng21 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Specifically, \cite{Deadbands, Sheng21 combine diffusion process modeling of power grid frequency with deadband control mechanisms to recover the empirical observation that the distribution of frequency can be bimodal \cite{Kraljic and develop numerical procedures to compute the partial differential equation which characterizes the frequency density.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: In our approach, we model the FCR-N volume as a control variable in the drift term, leading to an analytically tractable model that aligns with the unimodal frequency distribution observed empirically in the Nordic grid \cite{Kraljic .}
elements.sent-2411-042: A related body of literature examines dynamic dimensioning of ancillary services, as seen in works such as \cite{PoCBelgium, DDSweden, NSide .}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, these studies primarily address different ancillary services, such as aFRR or mFRR, whereas our focus is on FCR.
elements.sent-2411-044: Additionally, they employ distinct methodologies, such as linear programming approaches, rather than the probabilistic framework we adopt here.
elements.sent-2411-045: More broadly, probabilistic approaches to reserve dimensioning have been explored in \cite{Tsaousoglou, N1security, Zhang .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The remainder of this article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-047: In Section \ref{ProblemDescription , we provide a detailed description of the ancillary services involved in maintaining the 50 Hz grid frequency, with a focus on the specific requirements and dynamics of the Nordic synchronous area.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Section \ref{Modelling introduces the diffusion model that we use to capture the frequency development.}
elements.sent-2411-049: More specifically, we present foundational definitions in Subsection \ref{Definitions and derive closed-form approximations for exceedance probabilities in Subsection \ref{Theory , quantifying the likelihood of frequency deviations within specified ranges (e.g., between 49.5 and 49.9 Hz).}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In Section \ref{Data , we detail the real-world dataset, comprising over 300 million data points, used to calibrate our diffusion model (Subsection \ref{D M ), and we present our core findings through both visual and quantitative analyses (Subsection \ref{Results ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, in Section \ref{Outlook , we conclude with a discussion and an outlook for future research directions.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Proofs are presented in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this paper, we study how to dynamically dimension the FCR ancillary service.
elements.sent-2411-054: In particular, we examine the potential benefits and impact on grid security when transitioning from the current static procurement of FCR reserves to a dynamic dimensioning approach, where the procured volume of FCR reserves depends on the volatility of the frequency, assuming the TSO can predict those\footnote{Forecasting the volatility parameter would require dedicated research, as any dynamic approach depends on predictive modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Similar efforts are already ongoing in industry.
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, the Belgian company N-SIDE currently collaborates with the Nordic TSOs and is already working on hourly day-ahead predictions of imbalances in the context of dynamic dimensioning of aFRR/mFRR, based on the work they did for Elia, the Belgian TSO~\cite{NSide .. .}
elements.sent-2411-057: To assess the benefits of dynamic dimensioning, we compare the ``exceedance probabilities'', which quantify the likelihood that the frequency deviates beyond certain thresholds, e.g., $\pm 100$ mHz, from the target frequency of $50$ Hz at an arbitrary point in time.
elements.sent-2411-058: By examining these probabilities, we aim to determine how dynamic dimensioning affects grid stability in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-059: To support this analysis, in this section, we first provide a detailed overview of the relevant ancillary services, which will be captured in a mathematical model in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-060: We take the Nordic synchronous area as a case study; however, this approach can be adapted to other synchronous areas with potentially different ancillary services.
elements.sent-2411-061: As mentioned in Section~\ref{Intro , FCR in the Nordic synchronous area is differentiated into FCR-N for `normal' frequency conditions (i.e., frequencies between 49.9 and 50.1 Hz) and FCR-D for `disturbed' frequency conditions (i.e., frequencies below 49.9 Hz or above 50.1 Hz).}
elements.sent-2411-062: Additionally, FCR-D is further segmented by up-regulation and down-regulation requirements, leading to three distinct FCR markets within the Nordic region.
elements.sent-2411-063: FCR-N is sourced simultaneously for both up- and down-regulation, with a fixed obligation (i.e., to-be-procured volume) of 600 MW in both directions.
elements.sent-2411-064: This mechanism is designed to keep the exceedance probability outside of the normal band ($\pm 100$ mHz) to a maximum of 10,000 minutes per year ($\approx2\%$ of time)\cite{Entsoe .}
elements.sent-2411-065: FCR-N is activated proportionally to the frequency deviation from 50 Hz with a regulating strength of 6,000 MW/Hz between 49.9 and 50.1 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-066: Concretely, this means that 49.9 Hz corresponds to 600 MW of up-regulation and 50.1 Hz corresponds to 600 MW of down-regulation\cite{Entsoe2 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Providers of FCR-N are paid for both reservation and activation.
elements.sent-2411-068: The obligation for FCR-D Up is fixed at 1,450 MW, which is equal to the largest reference incident for up-regulation in the Nordics.
elements.sent-2411-069: FCR-D Up is activated proportionally to the frequency deviation from 50 Hz with a regulating strength of 3,625 MW/Hz between 49.5 and 49.9 Hz \cite{Entsoe2 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: The obligation for FCR-D Down is fixed at 1,400 MW, which is equal to the largest reference incident for down-regulation \cite{EnerginetOutlook .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Similarly to FCR-D Up, FCR-D Down is activated proportionally to the frequency deviation between 50.1 and 50.5 Hz, but then in the other direction.
elements.sent-2411-072: In the next section, where we model the FCR dynamics mathematically, we assume that FCR-D Up and FCR-D Down are sourced simultaneously in one market, similarly to FCR-N, ignoring the 50-MW difference for mathematical convenience.
elements.sent-2411-073: Finally, although our focus is on (dynamically) dimensioning FCR-N and establishing the probability of exceeding the $\pm 100$ mHz band, we also take into account the fixed obligation of FCR-D when modeling, since there is an obvious interplay between the two.
elements.sent-2411-074: Thus, we treat the fixed obligation of FCR-D as a (given) parameter in our model, while the FCR-N obligation is modeled as a (control) variable.
elements.sent-2411-075: A natural extension of this model is the one where the FCR-D obligation also becomes a control variable.
elements.sent-2411-076: We model the frequency development using a diffusion process that generalizes an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process \cite{Klebaner , \cite{BrowneWhitt with a controlled mean reversal parameter that represents the linear activation nature of the FCR-N and FCR-D ancillary services as a function of the frequency deviation, resulting in a piecewise constant drift.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: In Subsection \ref{Definitions , we define the necessary variables and introduce our model.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In Subsection \ref{Theory , we provide analytic expressions for the probability that the frequency is in a certain interval.}

elements.sent-2411-081: The evolution over time of shifted frequency $F(t)$ can then be modeled by the following diffusion process: \begin{equation}d{F}(t) = \mu(r_N;x_D,F(t),t)dt + \sigma(F(t),t)d{B}(t) \nonumber = -\alpha(r_N;x_D, F(t))F(t)dt + \sigma d{B}(t), \label{Diffproc}\end{equation} where $\alpha(r_N, x_D; F)$ is defined as \begin{equation}\alpha(r_N;x_D, F) = \nonumber \label{Alpha}  r_N + x_D if  F < -0.5 , or  F > 0.5 r_N - \frac{1}{4}x_D - \frac{10}{4} x_D F if  -0.5 \leq F \leq -0.1 -10 r_N F if  -0.1 \leq F \leq 0 10 r_N F if  0 \leq F \leq 0.1 r_N - \frac{1}{4} x_D + \frac{10}{4} x_D F if  0.1 \leq F \leq 0.5. \end{equation} Figure \ref{fig: alpha illustrates the drift \eqref{Alpha as a function of the frequency deviation.}}

elements.sent-2411-083: Since $-\alpha(\cdot; F) F <0$ if $F>0$ and $-\alpha(\cdot;F) F >0$ if $F<0$, it follows that the diffusion process \eqref{Diffproc is indeed mean-reverting.}

elements.sent-2411-086: Using the mathematical model introduced in the previous subsection, we are now interested in determining exceedance probabilities to assess the benefits and risk implications of changing from a static to a dynamic way of dimensioning FCR-N.
elements.sent-2411-087: In other words, for a particular hour, given an estimate of the diffusion coefficient $\sigma$ (describing the current volatility of the frequency signal), a fixed volume $x_D$ for the FCR-D reserve, and a chosen volume $r_N$ for the FCR-N obligation, we aim to calculate the probability that $F(\cdot)$ is in a certain frequency interval.
elements.sent-2411-088: As a qualitative metric, we look at the fraction of time the frequency exceeds a given threshold, which is related to the invariant or stationary distribution of the frequency.
elements.sent-2411-089: We now provide analytic expressions for the aforementioned probability under the assumption that stationarity of the frequency is achieved within an hour, which is the time period for which we intend to make decisions.

elements.sent-2411-106: We now give an overview of our main findings, which relate to the benefits and risk effects in terms of grid security, of the current static dimensioning approach versus the dynamic dimensioning approach with respect to the FCR-N services.
elements.sent-2411-107: We show that under a static dimensioning approach and with increasing volatility, one would need much more FCR-N volume than under a dynamic dimensioning approach to meet the same safety levels.
elements.sent-2411-108: We introduce several heuristics for the dynamic dimensioning approach that focus on deploying significantly more FCR-N reserves in high-volatility periods and much less in low-volatility periods.
elements.sent-2411-109: We demonstrate that this dimensioning approach results in substantially more effective use of the FCR-N budget than the current static one.
elements.sent-2411-110: We first study the current static dimensioning approach to understand the risk effects in terms of grid security of increasing volatility on the required FCR-N volumes.

elements.sent-2411-172: In this paper we took the Nordic grid as a case study and modeled the underlying frequency dynamics as a generalized Ornstein–Uhlenbeck (diffusion) process with non-constant drift.
elements.sent-2411-173: We used it to assess the reliability level of the grid, given the current volatility and available (static) FCR-N and FCR-D policies.
elements.sent-2411-174: We proposed and assessed multiple heuristic dynamic FCR-N policies and compared them with the current static one and showed that in general a dynamic dimensioning approach can offer more efficient and more effective allocation of primary reserves.
elements.sent-2411-175: Note that the relevance of our research expands to other synchronous areas, also those that are much larger and with higher traditional levels of inertia, such as continental Europe.
elements.sent-2411-176: Indeed, because of higher future penetration of renewables and hence less inertia, also in those areas, dynamic dimensioning of primary reserves such as FCR might become relevant.
elements.sent-2411-177: In our research, one of the underlying assumptions has been, amongst others, that one can perfectly forecast the standard deviation $\sigma$ of the frequency before activation of FCR-N and FCR-D on an hourly basis.
elements.sent-2411-178: Although, as mentioned, others have been doing work on predicting hourly imbalances \cite{PoCBelgium ,\cite{NSide , future research should explore the sensitivity of our results to the forecast error.}}
